sanskrit_devanagari:
कर्मण्यकर्म यः पश्येदकर्मणि च कर्म यः ।
स बुद्धिमान्मनुष्येषु स युक्तः कृत्स्नकर्मकृत् ॥ १८ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
karmaṇy akarma yaḥ paśyed akarmaṇi ca karma yaḥ |
sa buddhimān manuṣyeṣu sa yuktaḥ kṛtsna-karma-kṛt || 18 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: One who sees inaction in action and action in inaction is intelligent among men, and he is in the transcendental position, although engaged in all sorts of activities.
One who sees inaction in action, and action in inaction, is intelligent among men, and he is in the transcendental position, although engaged in all sorts of activities.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 994 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: A person acting in Kṛṣṇa consciousness is naturally free from the bonds of karma. His activities are all performed for Kṛṣṇa; therefore he does not enjoy or suffer any of the effects of work.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.18 Since engagement and non-engagement (in action) depend on an agent, therefore, yah, he who; pasyet, ie. pasyati, finds; akarma, inaction, absence of action; karmani, in action-karma means whatever is done, action in general; in tha action-; and yah, who; finds karma, action; akarmani, in inacti…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-18-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara famously reads this verse as resolving the action/inaction confusion through the doctrine of non-doership: in the actor who has realised the Self (which is non-acting), even visible action is metaphysically akarma — there is no doer. Conversely, in the ignorant person who abstains from physical action but whose mind is still filled with desires and intentions, that abstention is itself karma. The verse names the seeing that recognises both — the discriminative knowledge of the non-doing Self that grounds free action.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.18 Here by the term ‘non-action’, the knowledge of the self, which is distinct from action and which forms the subject under consideration, is meant. ‘He who sees non-action in action and also action in non-action,’ denotes him who can perceive knowledge of the self even while action is being perf…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-18-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse as resolving the confusion through the doctrine of unattached action: the karma-yogin who acts without ego (nirahaṅkāra) and without longing for fruits (niṣphala-kāma) sees that such action is, in the relevant sense, akarma — it does not bind. Conversely, the apparent abstainer whose mind is still engaged in desire-driven cognition is performing karma though appearing inactive. The seeing is recognition of agency-as-binding versus agency-as-unbinding.

disagreements_among_translators.word: akarma in karma / karma in akarma

positions.rendering: One who sees inaction in action and action in inaction is intelligent among men
Action with realisation of non-doership is metaphysically akarma; abstention with desire is metaphysically karma
Action without ego/desire is non-binding (akarma in the relevant sense); abstention with desire is itself binding karma

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The deepest doctrinal verse in chapter 4. Shankara reads the resolution metaphysically (non-doership of the Self). Ramanuja reads it phenomenologically (action without ego-and-desire is non-binding). Both readings agree: the surface (motion vs. stillness) does not disclose the doctrinal status of the action. The verse rewards a seeing that goes beyond surface.

literal_meaning: He who sees inaction in action and action in inaction — he is wise among humans; he is established in yoga, the doer of all action.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The chapter's headline verse and one of the most famous verses in the entire Gītā. Krishna names a single discriminative seeing in two directions: (1) seeing inaction (akarma) in apparent action (karma) — the actor who acts without doership; (2) seeing action (karma) in apparent inaction (akarma) — the apparent non-doer who is actually performing action (or whose 'inaction' is itself a positive karma). The one who sees both ways is buddhimān (wise) among humans, yukta (established in yoga), and — crucially — kṛtsna-karma-kṛt (the doer of all action). The verse rejects the equation of yoga with idleness: the right-seer is the most effective actor, not the least.
